metadata.working_title: Systemic pharmacological suppression of neural activity reverses learning impairment in a mouse model of Fragile X syndrome

abstract_sketch.problem: Enhancement of associative synaptic plasticity frequently impairs rather than enhances learning, and the mechanistic basis of this paradox remains unresolved, hindering clinical translation of plasticity-enhancing interventions.
abstract_sketch.gap: Previous work in MHCI KbDb-/- mice proposed that excessive plasticity saturates through aberrant recruitment by spontaneous activity, but this threshold metaplasticity hypothesis has not been tested across mouse lines with different molecular etiologies of enhanced plasticity, nor with a pharmacological intervention.
abstract_sketch.approach: In L7-Fmr1 KO mice (Purkinje cell-specific Fmr1 knockout; a Fragile X model with enhanced parallel fiber-Purkinje cell LTD), we tested oculomotor learning (VOR-increase, VOR-decrease, OKR adaptation) with and without behavioral pre-training, and with systemic diazepam pre-treatment 18-24 h prior to training.
abstract_sketch.key_results: L7-Fmr1 KO mice were selectively impaired on high-frequency VOR-increase learning (p=0.97, 0 vs 30 min; p=3.47x10-5 vs WT) and OKR adaptation (p=1.27x10-4 vs WT) while VOR-decrease learning was intact. Behavioral pre-training (p=0.001 after VOR-decrease pre-training) and diazepam pre-treatment 18-24 h before training (VOR-increase p=0.86 vs WT; OKR p=0.02 with KO enhanced over WT) eliminated the deficits; the rescue was transient, task-selective, and absent at low-frequency (0.5 Hz) VOR-increase training.
abstract_sketch.impact: These findings extend the threshold metaplasticity hypothesis to a second genetic model with distinct molecular origin, demonstrate that activity suppression constitutes a general and clinically accessible strategy for resetting circuits with pathologically enhanced LTD, and motivate repurposing of approved GABAergic drugs such as diazepam for learning disorders in autism and related conditions.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Long-term synaptic plasticity has been pursued as a therapeutic target, yet augmenting plasticity often impairs rather than enhances learning (Migaud et al. 1998; Uetani et al. 2000; Gu et al. 2002; Cox et al. 2003; Rutten et al. 2008; Navakkode et al. 2022), creating a conflicting literature that obstructs clinical translation.

Associative LTD at cerebellar PF-Purkinje cell synapses contributes selectively to certain cerebellum-dependent learning tasks; mouse lines with either impaired or enhanced PF-Purkinje cell LTD unexpectedly exhibit similar behavioral impairments on the same tasks.

Nguyen-Vu et al. (2017) proposed that enhanced LTD allows spontaneous circuit activity to saturate or raise the threshold for subsequent LTD induction, making plasticity unavailable where it is needed during training.
The hypothesis predicts that pre-training manipulations that reverse or prevent LTD in vivo should reset the circuit and restore learning, which was confirmed behaviorally in MHCI KbDb-/- mice.

Here we test two new predictions in L7-Fmr1 KO mice (a second genetic model with enhanced PF-Purkinje cell LTD, but acting through a distinct molecular pathway): (i) behavioral pre-training should rescue learning; and (ii) pharmacological suppression of neural activity with diazepam should rescue learning by preventing LTD induction during the pre-training period.

The experimental design situates the work within the Bienenstock-Cooper-Munro (BCM) sliding-threshold framework, extending threshold metaplasticity to a non-Hebbian, anti-Hebbian plasticity mechanism.

subsections.title: Selective learning impairment in L7-Fmr1 KO mice
content_bullets: Context: PF-Purkinje cell LTD is selectively implicated in VOR-increase and OKR adaptation but not VOR-decrease learning, so Fmr1 KO restricted to Purkinje cells provides a clean test of selectivity.
Approach: Paired vestibular (1 Hz, +/-10 deg/s) and oppositely or same-directed visual stimuli for 30 min to induce VOR-increase or VOR-decrease, in L7-Fmr1 KO vs WT littermates.

Finding: WT showed VOR-increase learning (p=7.37x10-4, 0 vs 30 min) and VOR-decrease learning (p=0.001). L7-Fmr1 KO mice showed no VOR-increase learning (p=0.97, 0 vs 30 min; p=3.47x10-5 vs WT at 30 min) but intact VOR-decrease learning (p=1.10x10-5, 0 vs 30 min; p=0.091 vs WT at 30 min). Deficits present in both sexes (male p=0.01; female p=0.05).
Finding: Baseline VOR was normal (p=0.95 vs WT in dark; p=0.50 during VOR-increase paired stimuli; p=0.76 during VOR-decrease paired stimuli), ruling out sensorimotor confounds.
Interpretation: Enhanced PF-Purkinje cell LTD selectively impairs the LTD-dependent form of VOR learning, consistent with MHCI KbDb-/- findings.

subsections.title: Behavioral pre-training eliminates the learning impairment
content_bullets: Context: If spontaneous activity aberrantly recruits LTD and raises its threshold, a manipulation designed to reverse LTD before training should reset the circuit and restore learning.
Approach: Applied 30 min of VOR-decrease pre-training or 30 min of Vestibular-only pre-training immediately before VOR-increase training in L7-Fmr1 KO and WT.

Finding: After VOR-decrease pre-training, L7-Fmr1 KO mice showed robust VOR-increase learning (p=0.001, 0 vs 30 min) indistinguishable from WT (p=0.17 for KO vs WT, t-test), despite the same cohort being non-learners without pre-training (p=0.99, 0 vs 30 min). Within-mouse comparison: p=0.01 paired t-test without vs with pre-training.
Finding: WT learning was not enhanced by pre-training (p=0.41 paired t-test), demonstrating that the intervention specifically rescues the KO phenotype.
Finding: Vestibular-only pre-training produced equivalent rescue (p=0.84 KO vs WT at 30 min).

Finding: Subsampling mice matched for pre-training-induced VOR-decrease magnitude preserved the rescue (p=0.74 after VOR-decrease pre-training; p=0.40 after Vestibular-only), excluding trivial carry-over from the pre-training-induced decrease.
Interpretation: Behavioral pre-training resets the PF-Purkinje cell circuit state to one compatible with subsequent LTD-dependent learning.

subsections.title: Diazepam pre-treatment rescues VOR-increase learning in L7-Fmr1 KO mice
content_bullets: Context: If activity-driven LTD induction causes the raised threshold, pharmacologically suppressing activity before training should also reset the circuit and restore learning.
Approach: Single IP injection of diazepam (0.5 mg/kg) 18-24 h before VOR-increase training, exploiting diazepam's ~24 h half-life and GABAergic suppression of cerebellar firing.
Finding (acute): Training 2 h after diazepam abolished VOR-increase learning in both WT and L7-Fmr1 KO (Fig 3-figure supplement 2), confirming drug action.

Finding (recovery window): 18-24 h post-diazepam, L7-Fmr1 KO mice learned robustly and were indistinguishable from WT (p=0.86, KO vs WT at 30 min). Same mice had no learning without drug (p=0.99, 0 vs 30 min).

Finding (specificity): No change in baseline VOR gain 2 h or 18-24 h post-diazepam (KO: p=0.72, p=0.77; WT: p=0.99, p=0.36). WT VOR-increase learning was unchanged by diazepam (p=0.55; p=0.99 across sessions). VOR-decrease learning unchanged (WT p=0.91; KO p=0.37; KO vs WT p=0.11).
Finding (transience): 1 week post-diazepam, L7-Fmr1 KO mice again failed to learn (p=0.12, 0 vs 30 min).

Interpretation: A single subclinical dose of a GABAergic modulator transiently restores LTD-dependent learning, consistent with activity-limited metaplastic reset rather than persistent circuit remodeling.

subsections.title: Low-frequency VOR-increase learning impairment is not rescued
content_bullets: Context: Prior work indicates PF-Purkinje cell LTD contributes selectively to high-frequency (>= 1 Hz), not low-frequency (<= 0.66 Hz) VOR learning, affording a double-dissociation test.
Approach: Replicated the VOR-increase protocol using 0.5 Hz stimuli and applied both behavioral pre-training and diazepam interventions.

Finding: Low-frequency VOR-increase learning trended lower in L7-Fmr1 KO (p=0.06 vs WT at 30 min). Neither VOR-decrease pre-training (p=0.47), Vestibular-only pre-training (p=0.35), nor diazepam pre-treatment (p=0.66) rescued the low-frequency deficit.

Interpretation: The pre-training-reversible deficit maps specifically onto the LTD-dependent component of VOR learning, supporting the hypothesis that these interventions act through LTD-related metaplasticity rather than a general enhancement of plasticity. Residual low-frequency impairment may reflect off-target effects of Fmr1 loss.

subsections.title: Diazepam rescues OKR adaptation, the most LTD-dependent oculomotor task
content_bullets: Context: OKR adaptation is the oculomotor task with the strongest evidence for obligatory PF-Purkinje cell LTD, and therefore the strongest predicted rescue by diazepam.
Approach: 60 min of 1 Hz optokinetic drum training (+/-10 deg/s) 18-24 h after saline or diazepam in L7-Fmr1 KO and WT.

Finding: WT showed robust OKR adaptation (p=0.001, 0 vs 60 min). L7-Fmr1 KO showed no OKR adaptation post-saline (p=0.87, 0 vs 60 min; p=1.27x10-4 vs WT). 18-24 h post-diazepam, L7-Fmr1 KO OKR learning was rescued (saline vs diazepam in KO: p=0.0001 at 60 min) and exceeded WT (p=0.02, KO vs WT at 60 min).
Finding: Baseline OKR gain was unchanged by genotype (p=0.69 Pre) or diazepam (KO p=0.69; WT p=0.55).
Interpretation: The most LTD-dependent task shows the strongest, above-WT rescue, providing the most stringent confirmation of the threshold metaplasticity prediction.

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: Paragraph 1 (framing): Reiterate that enhancement of plasticity impairs learning via the threshold metaplasticity mechanism, now tested in a second, molecularly distinct genetic model.

Paragraph 2 (convergent evidence): Shared phenotypes in L7-Fmr1 KO and MHCI KbDb-/- mice, despite Fmr1 acting via mGluR-dependent translation and MHCI via MAP kinase/integrin pathways, implicate the shared downstream enhancement of LTD rather than off-target molecular effects.

Paragraph 3 (task-specificity): The selective sparing of VOR-decrease learning and selective rescue of high-frequency but not low-frequency VOR-increase learning dissect LTD-dependent from LTD-independent cerebellar learning, strengthening causal inference.

Paragraph 4 (clinical translation): Diazepam is FDA-approved and produced complete rescue without side effects on other oculomotor behaviors. Pre-training activity suppression is generalizable to any cerebellar (or potentially extra-cerebellar) region with pathologically enhanced LTD, in contrast to behavioral pre-training which requires task-specific design.

Paragraph 5 (theoretical extension): Results extend the BCM sliding-threshold framework to anti-Hebbian (associative LTD) plasticity, where threshold metaplasticity cannot serve homeostatic firing-rate stability and instead may limit plasticity, protect recent memories (Fusi et al. 2005), and allocate memories across distinct Purkinje cells. Threshold shifts appear driven by history of plasticity induction rather than firing rates per se.

Paragraph 6 (scope and limits): Fmr1 deletion produces impairments beyond LTD-dependent learning (low-frequency VOR-increase), indicating that Fragile X phenotypes combine enhanced LTD with additional Purkinje-cell deficits; identifying candidate mechanisms for threshold metaplasticity (Ohtsuki et al. 2012; Hansel and Linden 2000; Hunley et al. 2023; Rowan et al. 2018) remains an open direction.

sections.title: Methods
content_bullets: Mice: L7/Pcp2-Cre x Fmr1 conditional floxed crosses producing Purkinje-cell-specific Fmr1 KO; Cre-negative littermates as WT. Ages 8-22 weeks, both sexes pooled (similar deficits in both).
Surgery: Head-post implant and conjunctival magnetic eye-tracker magnets; recovery >=5 days.

Oculomotor testing: Vestibular turntable at 0.5 or 1 Hz sinusoidal, +/-10 deg/s; optokinetic drum with alternating black/white stripes (~7.5 deg each). Testing and training blocks as described, 10-min training blocks x 3 = 30 min total.
OKR adaptation: 60 x 50-s training blocks (with 10 s dark) at 1 Hz, +/-10 deg/s.
Drug administration: IP diazepam at 0.4, 0.5, or 2.5 mg/kg, or saline; training 2 h, 18-24 h, or 1 week post-injection; pseudorandomized order.

Eye-movement analysis: Savitzky-Golay differentiation, 4th-order 15 Hz Butterworth filter, sinusoidal fitting with 31 deg/s outlier exclusion, 45% data exclusion threshold; VOR learning = % change relative to pre-training baseline; OKR learning = % change from first 3 to last 3 of 60 training blocks.
Statistics: Shapiro-Wilk normality, two-factor repeated-measures ANOVA with post-hoc Tukey, or two-sample / paired t-tests; OriginPro 2022; alpha = 0.05; mean +/- SEM reported.

figure_plan.title: VOR-increase learning is selectively impaired in L7-Fmr1 KO mice
figure_plan.content: Two-panel bar chart comparing WT vs L7-Fmr1 KO VOR-increase vs VOR-decrease learning at 30 min. Uses extracted p-values.

figure_plan.content: Three conditions (no pre-training, VOR-decrease pre-training, Vestibular-only pre-training) x genotype.

figure_plan.title: Low-frequency (0.5 Hz) VOR-increase impairment is not rescued
